critic_raw:
Section 10 explicitly states: 'the test set metrics are briefly consulted during model training to verify consistency across train and validation periods before finalizing the model.' This is direct test set contamination — using test set metrics to decide whether to finalize or adjust the model violates holdout integrity regardless of whether the 'consultation' is labeled as brief.

The stratified random split for 36-month sensor time-series data mixes temporal windows from the same equipment units across train and test sets. Equipment on a degradation trajectory will have overlapping 7-day prediction windows in both sets — the RNN/XGBoost models are effectively tested on states from the same degradation episode they were trained on, producing optimistically inflated test metrics.

The primary model predicts time-to-failure as a continuous regression target, then binarizes predictions at a threshold optimized on validation data. This threshold optimization is an additional hyperparameter that could overfit to the validation class balance. The decision to frame the problem as regression and then binarize is also not motivated — it adds a conversion step that introduces error. A directly trained classifier would be cleaner.

The rule-based baseline (maintenance schedule) predicts failure based on elapsed time since last maintenance, not based on sensor data. This compares sensor-driven ML against schedule-driven maintenance, not against another sensor-based statistical model. The hypothesis states prediction of 'machine failures more accurately than traditional maintenance schedules' — the design conflates 'schedule-based' with 'statistical methods' even though they are distinct. There is no statistical sensor-data baseline (e.g., CUSUM or SPC control charts), only LR.

The primary metric 'Downtime Cost Reduction' is a formula with assumed constants (8 hours prevented, 2 hours wasted, 48 hours unplanned) that are not derived from plant data. This metric is a pseudo-economic measure whose sign and magnitude depend critically on these constants — changing the assumed average prevented downtime from 8 to 4 hours can reverse model rankings. Reporting this as the primary metric with unstated sensitivity to the constants is misleading.
all_issues_raised: Section 10 states test set metrics are 'briefly consulted during model training to verify consistency before finalizing' — this is explicit test set contamination that invalidates the holdout evaluation.

Stratified random split for 36-month sensor time-series data allows equipment degradation trajectories to span training and test sets — temporally proximal sensor windows from the same equipment failure episode appear in both sets.

The primary model is a regression-then-binarize pipeline where the binarization threshold is an additional hyperparameter tuned on validation data — the two-stage approach introduces unnecessary error conversion without motivation over a directly trained classifier.

The rule-based baseline uses elapsed-time scheduling, not sensor statistics — this compares ML against schedule-driven maintenance, not against a statistical sensor-data model, making the comparison test a different claim than stated in the hypothesis.

The 'Downtime Cost Reduction' primary metric uses assumed constants (8/2/48 hours) not derived from plant data — the metric's sign and magnitude are highly sensitive to these assumptions, and results reported with unexplored constants are not interpretable.
all_issues_adjudicated: Section 10 states test set metrics are 'briefly consulted during model training to verify consistency before finalizing' — this is explicit test set contamination that invalidates the holdout evaluation.

Stratified random split for 36-month sensor time-series data allows equipment degradation trajectories to span training and test sets — temporally proximal sensor windows from the same equipment failure episode appear in both sets.

The primary model is a regression-then-binarize pipeline where the binarization threshold is an additional hyperparameter tuned on validation data — the two-stage approach introduces unnecessary error conversion without motivation over a directly trained classifier.

The rule-based baseline uses elapsed-time scheduling, not sensor statistics — this compares ML against schedule-driven maintenance, not against a statistical sensor-data model, making the comparison test a different claim than stated in the hypothesis.

The 'Downtime Cost Reduction' primary metric uses assumed constants (8/2/48 hours) not derived from plant data — the metric's sign and magnitude are highly sensitive to these assumptions, and results reported with unexplored constants are not interpretable.
